metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC6's lint assertion with scope: either (a) scope the lint check (f) to the three fragments this story adds (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp`) and add a comment that `evidence-artifacts` will be wired when Finding 3 lands, or (b) explicitly declare `evidence-artifacts` as a stub/placeholder step in this story so all four names are present in release.yml from day one.
findings.finding: AC6 names four artifact fragments in the release-lint.yml check (f) — `evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts` — but the AC text and the scope section state that `evidence-artifacts` (wheel/sdist digests) is out of scope for this story and will be added by Finding 3. If the lint check asserts all four names in release.yml and this story doesn't add `evidence-artifacts`, the lint check will fail in PR CI, making the end-state unreachable within this story's scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6; Scope section 'release-lint.yml'; What section 'Print and retain release evidence'
findings.suggested_fix: In the release-lint.yml check (f), assert only the three fragment names this story delivers: `evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp`. Add a TODO comment noting that `evidence-artifacts` is wired by Finding 3. Update AC6 text to match: 'the three fragment names this story delivers … appear in release.yml; the consolidation step is present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the evidence fragment list across the What section, Scope check (f), and AC 6: either add `evidence-artifacts` explicitly to AC 6's fragment list (with a note that it is this story's responsibility) or move it out of the What/Scope descriptions and into Finding 3's scope, and update Scope check (f) to match the definitive count.
findings.finding: The evidence artifact fragment count is contradictory between the 'What' section / Scope lint check (f) and AC 6: 'What' and Scope list four fragments (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`, `evidence-artifacts`) while AC 6's text lists only three (`evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`), omitting `evidence-artifacts`. An implementer cannot determine from the plan whether `evidence-artifacts` is in scope for this story or belongs to Finding 3's build-once step.
findings.location: AC 6 ('Release evidence records…') vs What section ('each job uploads its own distinctly-named fragment') and Scope section 'release-lint.yml' check (f)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC 6, change the fragment list to `evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`/`evidence-artifacts` (four) and add a parenthetical clarifying that `evidence-artifacts` is this story's job (e.g., the artifact-listing step), distinct from Finding 3's wheel/sdist digest fragment. Alternatively, if `evidence-artifacts` truly belongs to Finding 3, remove it from the What section's fragment list and from Scope check (f)'s four-name grep pattern, and update check (f) to match three names.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `gerrit-verify.yaml` to the Scope and specify how the `release-lint.yml` checks (SHA-pin grep, persist-credentials grep, --no-isolation grep, permission-structure assertion, evidence-wiring assertion) are invoked in the Gerrit pre-merge gate — either by adding an inline step to `gerrit-verify.yaml` or by extracting the checks into a reusable workflow callable from both.
findings.finding: The new `release-lint.yml` workflow is PR-triggered (on changes to `release.yml` / `docs/releasing.md`), but Gerrit pre-merge changes dispatch `gerrit-verify.yaml` via `workflow_dispatch` — GitHub PR triggers never fire for Gerrit-landed changes. This means AC1/AC2/AC4/AC5/AC6 enforcement (SHA-pin lint, persist-credentials, --no-isolation, permission structure, evidence wiring) is entirely bypassed in the Gerrit gate, and any regression to `release.yml` introduced by a Gerrit change would not be caught pre-merge.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/workflows/release-lint.yml` (new) / `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the evidence-fragment list: either update the Scope lint check (f) regex to assert only three fragments (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp`) to match AC6's text and out-of-scope ruling, or update AC6 to include `evidence-artifacts` as in-scope for this story and remove the deferral note.
findings.finding: The evidence-fragment count is contradicted internally between plan sections: the 'What' section and the Scope's lint check (f) regex (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts`) list FOUR fragments including `evidence-artifacts`, but AC6 text names only THREE fragments (`evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`) and explicitly defers `evidence-artifacts` (wheel/sdist digests) as out-of-scope to Finding 3. This means the lint check (f) as written in the Scope would assert for a fragment that AC6 says this story does NOT produce.
findings.location: Scope section / lint check (f) vs. AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope lint check (f) regex to `evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp` (three fragments) and note that `evidence-artifacts` is asserted by Finding 3's story after it lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the evidence fragment name enum: pick a single canonical count (3 or 4 fragments) and make the 'What' narrative, Scope lint-check (f) regex, AC6 prose, and the consolidation job spec all agree on exactly the same set of fragment names.
findings.finding: The evidence artifact fragment name enum is defined differently in two plan sections: the 'What' section and Scope lint check (f) list FOUR fragments (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`, `evidence-artifacts`), while AC6's body text and AC6's list of fragment names name only THREE (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`), omitting `evidence-artifacts`. An implementer writing the consolidation job's `download-artifact` steps and the lint-check regex has two contradictory canonical sources with no tie-break rule.
findings.location: 'What' section (Print and retain release evidence), Scope (lint check (f)), AC6 body text
findings.suggested_fix: Decide whether `evidence-artifacts` is in scope for this story (wheel/sdist digests belong to Finding 3 per AC6's own note). If it is out of scope, remove it from the 'What' section and Scope lint regex so all three references name the same three fragments (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`). If it is in scope, add it back to AC6's prose and define which job produces it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add pip-tools to the plan's scope as an explicit prerequisite: specify where it is installed (e.g. as a dev dependency in `pyproject.toml` or as a pinned `pip install pip-tools==<exact>` step in the docs), and note how pip-tools itself is version-pinned to avoid the bootstrap circularity.
findings.finding: The plan designates `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file generation command and hard-codes `grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' docs/releasing.md` as a CI assertion, but pip-tools is not installed anywhere in the repo (not in `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies, no CI workflow references it). The plan does not specify how pip-tools itself is installed for the lock-regeneration step — creating a bootstrap gap: a developer who wants to regenerate `.github/release-requirements.txt` must separately discover and install pip-tools. If `pip-compile` is not available in the CI environment that runs `release-lint.yml`, the static assertion (`grep` check) would pass even though the docs command is not executable in that environment. Evidence class: Partially-verified (pip-compile --generate-hashes flags are real, but the installation precondition in CI/developer workflow is unspecified).
findings.location: Scope / `.github/release-requirements.txt` bullet / AC3 / docs AC grep check
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools` as a dev dependency in `pyproject.toml` (e.g. `pip-tools>=7` under `[project.optional-dependencies] dev`) so it is installed in the standard dev setup. Document the exact version in `docs/releasing.md`. Alternatively, provide a one-liner `pip install pip-tools==<pinned-version>` in the 'Pinning' section docs, explicitly noting this is the only tool that bootstraps outside the hash-locked env.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the release-lint.yml check (f) to assert each fragment name appears as the value of an `upload-artifact` `name:` field (e.g., `grep -E "name: evidence-shapin" release.yml`), not merely that the string appears anywhere in the file.
findings.finding: The plan uses a pipe-separated grep pattern to assert fragment-name presence (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts`) but never specifies per-name cardinality. A single line in release.yml that concatenates all four names (e.g. in a comment or a single `echo`) would satisfy the grep while the actual upload-artifact steps remain absent — a substring false-positive per VCDT defect #1/#5.
findings.location: Scope section '.github/workflows/release-lint.yml' check (f); AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the pipe-or grep with four separate anchored greps: `grep -E "name:\s*evidence-shapin" .github/workflows/release.yml`, repeated for each fragment name. This ensures the names appear in the correct structural position.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing, verify that each action tag in `release.yml` (`actions/checkout@v7`, `actions/setup-python@v6`, `actions/upload-artifact@v7`, `actions/download-artifact@v8`) resolves to a real commit SHA on GitHub (e.g. `gh api /repos/actions/checkout/git/ref/refs/tags/v7`); if any tag does not exist, update the plan to use the correct existing tag (likely v4 for all four) before the SHA-pin step.
findings.finding: The action version tags currently in `release.yml` — `actions/checkout@v7` (lines 31, 124), `actions/setup-python@v6` (line 32), `actions/upload-artifact@v7` (line 43), `actions/download-artifact@v8` (lines 60, 85) — exceed known published major versions for those actions (the latest published major versions are checkout@v4, setup-python@v5, upload-artifact@v4, download-artifact@v4). If these tags do not exist as real refs in the respective action repositories, the SHA-pinning step cannot resolve a commit SHA for any of them, making the core deliverable of this plan (full-SHA action pins) infeasible as described. The plan's entire AC1 rests on successfully resolving these tags to SHAs. Evidence class: Unverified — cannot probe GitHub from this environment, but the tag versions are anomalously high.
findings.location: release.yml lines 31, 32, 43, 60, 85, 124 / AC1 / What section
findings.suggested_fix: Run `gh api /repos/actions/{checkout,setup-python,upload-artifact,download-artifact}/git/ref/refs/tags/v{7,6,7,8}` for each action to confirm the tags exist. If they do not (the latest known major for all four is v4), correct `release.yml` to use the highest existing tag first, then SHA-pin those. Update the plan's Why section references (lines 31/32/43/60/85) accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies (or document its installation via `pipx`) and name the install step in the Scope so that both local lock-file regeneration and any CI validation step that invokes `pip-compile` have a grounded install path.
findings.finding: `pip-compile` (from `pip-tools`) is mandated as the lock-file generation tool throughout the plan (Scope: 'compiled from .github/release-requirements.in via pip-compile --generate-hashes'; AC3; docs AC grep check), but `pip-tools` is not listed as a new addition to `pyproject.toml` dev deps and no CI install step for it is named in the Scope — leaving its installation undefined for both developers and CI.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/release-requirements.txt` bullet / AC3 / docs AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools>=7` to the `[dev]` extras in `pyproject.toml` and update `docs/releasing.md`'s 'Pinning' section to include `pip install pip-tools` (or `pipx install pip-tools`) as a prerequisite for the `pip-compile --generate-hashes …` command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'What' section's install command to include `--no-deps` (`pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`) so both the narrative and the AC specify the mechanically-correct invocation.
findings.finding: The 'What' section body specifies the hash-locked install as `pip install --require-hashes -r .github/release-requirements.txt` (omitting `--no-deps`), but AC3 correctly includes it as `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`. Without `--no-deps`, pip will attempt to resolve and install transitive dependencies not listed in the lock file — without hash verification for those packages — silently bypassing the hash-lock for unlisted transitive deps. The mechanism only works correctly with `--no-deps` (requiring all transitive deps to be explicitly listed in the lock). An implementer following the 'What' section would produce a broken lock enforcement.
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file…'; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, change `pip install --require-hashes -r .github/release-requirements.txt` to `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` and add a note that `--no-deps` is required because `--require-hashes` only checks packages in the requirements file, so all transitive deps must be explicitly listed in the lock (which `pip-compile --generate-hashes` does automatically).

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the plan for choosing pip-tools over `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (or flip the designation if uv is preferred), and confirm the AC7 docs-grep command reflects whichever tool is canonical — to avoid hardcoding a tool choice into ACs without documented justification.
findings.finding: pip-tools (`pip-compile`) is introduced as a net-new tool dependency for generating `.github/release-requirements.txt`, but has zero existing call-sites in the repo (no `pip-compile`, no `pip-tools` reference anywhere in the codebase). The plan acknowledges `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as an alternative but then designates pip-tools as canonical — embedding the pip-tools command string directly into the docs AC grep check (`grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' docs/releasing.md`), hardcoding the tool choice into the AC's verification mechanism. Neither pip-tools nor uv is currently used as a lock-file generator; introducing pip-tools as the designated tool without a stated rationale for choosing it over the already-mentioned uv alternative adds an undiscussed new tool dependency.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/release-requirements.in` / `.github/release-requirements.txt`; What section 'pip-compile --generate-hashes'; AC3; AC7 (docs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the aggregation step validates each downloaded fragment is non-empty (e.g., `[ -s <fragment-file> ] || { echo 'ERROR: fragment <name> missing'; exit 1; }`) before concatenation, so an incomplete evidence artifact causes a hard workflow failure rather than a silent gap.
findings.finding: The evidence aggregation step (final consolidation job) is not specified to fail loudly when one or more fragment artifacts (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`, `evidence-artifacts`) are missing. If an upstream job's `upload-artifact` call fails silently or the artifact name is mistyped, the aggregation step may `download-artifact` zero files for that fragment and silently produce an incomplete `release-evidence.txt` — the release proceeds with a partial audit trail.
findings.location: AC 6 (release evidence); Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml` aggregation step; `release-lint.yml` check (f)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add retry/timeout to the MCP publisher `curl` download step (e.g., `curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --max-time 60 -fsSL`) and document in the plan that re-triggering the release workflow is safe/idempotent if the download fails before sha256sum verification.
findings.finding: The `curl` download of the MCP publisher archive in `mcp_verify` has no retry or backoff specified. If the GitHub Releases endpoint is transiently unavailable (rate-limit, CDN blip, DNS timeout), the job fails immediately and the entire release must be re-triggered manually. The plan specifies `curl -fsSL` (which is correct for non-2xx failure) but says nothing about retries.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download steps (1) curl / (2) sha256sum / (3) tar; AC 5
findings.suggested_fix: Update the MCP publisher download step in the plan to: `curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --connect-timeout 15 --max-time 120 -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` and add a note that re-running the workflow from scratch is safe because the sha256sum check is the commit gate — no state has been mutated if the download failed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a PR-CI behavioral test (e.g., in `release-lint.yml` or a dedicated `test-release-build.yml`) that installs a minimal Python environment, runs `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` in a clean venv, and asserts exit 0 — so the requirements file's validity is verified on every PR that touches it, not only at release time.
findings.finding: The hash-locked build path — `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` followed by `python -m build --no-isolation` — has no behavioral PR-CI test. The only in-CI validation is a structural grep in release-lint.yml that checks for the literal string `--no-isolation` in `release.yml`. A malformed or incorrect `.github/release-requirements.txt` (wrong hashes, a missing transitive dep, a typo in a package name) would silently pass all PR CI gates and only be discovered when a real release run fails.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 3 (hash-locked install) and AC 4 (--no-isolation enforcement); Scope — `.github/release-requirements.txt`; `.github/workflows/release-lint.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: In `.github/workflows/release-lint.yml`, add a job (e.g., `validate-release-requirements`) triggered on changes to `.github/release-requirements.txt` or `.github/release-requirements.in` that: (1) checks out the repo, (2) sets up the same Python version used in `release.yml`, (3) creates a fresh venv (`python -m venv .venv`), and (4) runs `.venv/bin/pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`. This exercises the actual hash-verification path in PR CI without requiring a real release event. Note: `--dry-run` is insufficient per the plan's own rationale; use a real install into a fresh venv.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an edit to `docs/releasing.md` 'Supply-chain attestations' section (line 45) to replace the mutable `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference with the SHA-pinned form (or a note directing readers to `release.yml` for the authoritative pin), so the policy-establishing story doesn't leave a contradictory mutable reference in-place.
findings.finding: `docs/releasing.md` line 45 (in the 'Supply-chain attestations' section, outside the plan's claimed 'Pinning' section ownership) quotes `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` verbatim — a mutable tag. After this story SHA-pins that action in `release.yml`, the docs reference becomes a policy contradiction that the plan does not touch.
findings.location: Scope section / docs/releasing.md line 45 ('Supply-chain attestations' section)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Supply-chain attestations' section, replace the literal `@release/v1` reference with `@<sha>  # release/v1` to match the pinned form used in `release.yml`, or add a note: 'See release.yml for the pinned SHA — the tag shown here is for readability only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a re-digest check (`echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict`) in `mcp_registry` immediately after `download-artifact` and before `chmod +x` / `./mcp-publisher login`, so the OIDC-privileged job independently verifies the binary it is about to execute.
findings.finding: The mcp_registry job executes the mcp-publisher binary (downloaded from artifact storage) without re-verifying its digest. The sha256sum check runs in mcp_verify against the *archive* before extraction, but what crosses the artifact-store boundary is the extracted binary. If the binary in artifact storage were substituted (e.g., by a storage-layer issue or implementation bug), the OIDC-privileged job would execute the substitute without detection. The plan's security claim — 'the OIDC scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary' — is only airtight if the binary digest is also re-checked in mcp_registry after download-artifact.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the mcp_registry job, after `actions/download-artifact`, add: `echo "<same-pinned-sha256-as-mcp_verify>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` — using the SHA-256 of the extracted binary (not the archive). Document in the plan that the pinned digest stored for mcp_verify is the archive SHA-256 and a second binary SHA-256 is computed at pin time for mcp_registry's check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step in `mcp_registry` that re-verifies the SHA-256 of the downloaded `mcp-publisher` binary (using the same pinned digest) immediately after `download-artifact` and before `chmod +x` / execution, so the OIDC-privileged job never executes an artifact whose digest it has not locally confirmed.
findings.finding: The plan's stated security contract — 'the OIDC-privileged job never downloads or holds an unverified binary' — relies on the integrity of the GitHub Actions artifact store as the handoff channel between `mcp_verify` and `mcp_registry`, but `mcp_registry` does not re-verify the SHA-256 of the artifact after `download-artifact` before executing it. The plan's own 'Fail on an unexpected filename/size/digest/version' requirement is only scoped to the `mcp_verify` job's download step, leaving the post-handoff execution path without an explicit verification assertion.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher split / AC 5 (`mcp_registry` consumes verified artifact)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `mcp_registry` job, after `actions/download-artifact`, add: `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` before `chmod +x mcp-publisher`. Reference the same pinned digest constant used in `mcp_verify`. Update AC 5 and the `release-lint.yml` check (e) to assert this re-verification step is present in the `mcp_registry` job.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the '/ sets a `verified` output' alternative from the `mcp_verify` job description in the What section, leaving only 'uploads the verified binary as a named artifact' to match the AC's unambiguous requirement.
findings.finding: The `mcp_verify` job description uses 'uploads the verified binary as an artifact / sets a `verified` output' with a slash suggesting two alternatives are both acceptable, but the `mcp_registry` AC pin unambiguously to artifact consumption ('consumes only the verified artifact'). An executor could implement only the output-variable path (no artifact upload), satisfy the slash, and break the AC.
findings.location: What section 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute'; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'uploads the verified binary as an artifact / sets a `verified` output' to 'uploads the verified binary as a named artifact (`mcp-publisher`) so the OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job can download it without re-fetching from the network.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the rationale for (a) `--no-isolation` as the Hatchling enforcement mechanism and (b) the structural mcp_verify/mcp_registry job split as the OIDC isolation boundary, so the security model is durable beyond the PR.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two non-obvious architectural decisions — (1) using `--no-isolation` to enforce Hatchling's version via the pre-installed locked env rather than pyproject.toml's isolated build, and (2) the structural job-split security contract (mcp_verify + mcp_registry) to isolate OIDC scope — with detailed rationale in the plan body but no corresponding ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update. The `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' section covers only the operational how-to (commands, update process). Future maintainers encountering an unexpected `--no-isolation` flag or the two-job MCP structure have no durable committed record of why these choices were made.
findings.location: Scope → `docs/releasing.md` section; What → '--no-isolation' and 'Security contract — structural job split' subsections
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0XXX-release-supply-chain-pinning.md` (or an equivalent `## Architecture` subsection in `docs/releasing.md`) covering: why `--no-isolation` is chosen over a `[build-system]` pin, why a job split (not step ordering) is required for OIDC isolation, and what would need to change if the build strategy evolves. Reference this from the 'Pinning' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a minimal extensibility pattern for the arch-specific MCP publisher URL+SHA pairs (e.g., a commented `# ARCH_PINS: linux_amd64=<url>|<sha256>` block or a small env-var map) so future OS/arch additions have a prescribed location and format.
findings.finding: The arch-specific MCP publisher URL + SHA-256 pair is hard-coded for `linux/amd64` (the current `ubuntu-latest` runner), and the plan acknowledges future arch expansion ('store one pair per supported OS/arch tuple if the matrix ever expands') but defines no configuration structure for that expansion — no env-var map, no matrix lookup, no comment template. When a second OS/arch is needed, the implementer has no prescribed pattern, increasing the risk of ad-hoc, inconsistent handling.
findings.location: What → 'Arch note' subsection; AC 5 (MCP publisher AC)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a comment block in `release.yml` above the MCP publisher download step of the form:
```yaml
# MCP publisher pins (one entry per OS/arch; update via reviewed PR):
# linux_amd64: url=<url> sha256=<sha256>
env:
  MCP_URL: "<linux_amd64_url>"
  MCP_SHA256: "<linux_amd64_sha256>"
```
This gives a clear, discoverable location for future arch entries without requiring a new file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the SHA-pin (AC1), persist-credentials (AC2), --no-isolation (AC4), and permission-structure (AC5) assertions in `release-lint.yml` should instead be implemented as new assertions in `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (or a sibling `tests/unit/test_release_workflow.py`), avoiding a duplicate 'parse workflow YAML and grep for signatures' mechanism alongside the existing Python test fixture.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke `release-lint.yml` grep/shell workflow to enforce SHA-pin, persist-credentials, and --no-isolation constraints in `release.yml`, rebuilding (in a narrower, shell-grep form) the same 'read a workflow YAML file and assert a signature is present' pattern already implemented in `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py`. That test file reads workflow files via Python and asserts string signatures; the same mechanism could directly add `release.yml` SHA-pin and `persist-credentials: false` assertions as new entries in `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` or a parallel `_RELEASE_GATE_SIGNATURES` dict, routing through the existing `make test` / Gerrit Verified gate rather than a net-new CI workflow.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/workflows/release-lint.yml` (new); AC1, AC2, AC4, AC6; tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` that reads `.github/workflows/release.yml` (same pattern as `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`) and asserts: all `uses:` match a 40-hex SHA regex; all `actions/checkout` blocks contain `persist-credentials: false`; the build invocation contains `--no-isolation`; `mcp_verify` has no `id-token: write`; `mcp_registry` does. These run through `make test` → Gerrit Verified gate automatically, eliminating the need for a separate `release-lint.yml` workflow for those checks. Reserve `release-lint.yml` only for the docs-section grep checks that genuinely need a PR-triggered workflow (e.g., asserting `docs/releasing.md` content).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the 'What' section explaining why the bespoke grep approach is preferred over tools like `zizmor`/`actionlint` for this use case (e.g., because custom checks — `--no-isolation`, permission-structure assertions, evidence-fragment names — are outside those tools' rule sets, and the narrow release.yml scope makes a purpose-built check simpler to own).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke `release-lint.yml` grep/shell workflow to enforce SHA-pin, persist-credentials, --no-isolation, permission structure, and evidence-fragment constraints, but provides no positive rationale for why this approach is preferred over established GitHub Actions security linters (e.g., zizmor, actionlint). G6 requires the plan to state a positive rationale for the chosen approach; its absence is a (minor) finding. The bespoke approach IS defensible (it handles custom checks no off-shelf tool covers), but the rationale should be stated explicitly so implementers and reviewers understand why a maintained alternative was not adopted.
findings.location: What section — '.github/workflows/release-lint.yml (new …)'; Scope section; AC1, AC2, AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` releases include a `checksums.txt` or equivalent (e.g. by checking the GitHub Releases page for a recent version), and update the plan to state the actual finding; if no checksums file exists, remove the 'prefer upstream' branch and go directly to the hard-coded SHA-256 path with an explicit update procedure.
findings.finding: The plan's MCP publisher verification step asserts 'Prefer an upstream `checksums.txt` if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one', but the plan provides no evidence that such a file exists or has been observed in the upstream release assets. If the upstream does not ship a `checksums.txt`, the fallback is hard-coding a SHA-256 computed at pin time — which is feasible, but the primary path's existence is unverified. More critically, the plan does not specify what happens when the pinned exact version is updated: who re-computes the SHA-256, what command to use, and whether the update PR must include the new checksums.txt line or a hard-coded value. Evidence class: Unverified for the checksums.txt path; Partially-verified for the hard-coded fallback.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' / AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'artifact / sets a `verified` output' OR in the mcp_verify→mcp_registry handoff spec: pick exactly one inter-job mechanism (upload-artifact for the binary, OR a job output flag + independent re-download + re-verify in mcp_registry) and make the AC, the chmod+x requirement, and the lint-check (f) assertion all describe the same chosen mechanism.
findings.finding: The `mcp_verify`→`mcp_registry` inter-job handoff protocol is specified as an OR ('uploads the verified binary as an artifact / sets a `verified` output') but the plan never resolves which mechanism is required. `upload-artifact`+`download-artifact` and job `outputs` + `needs.<job>.outputs` are mutually exclusive YAML constructs with different size limits, different security boundaries, and different syntax — an implementer cannot write either job's YAML without choosing one.
findings.location: 'What' section (Security contract — structural job split), AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Commit to upload-artifact (the binary handoff path): replace '/ sets a `verified` output' with ', uploading it as the `mcp-publisher-verified` artifact'. Then AC5 and lint check (f) can assert the specific artifact name exists, and the chmod+x requirement is unambiguously attached to the download-artifact step in mcp_registry.

findings.finding: AC6's evidence-concatenation outcome is only verifiable during an actual release run; no PR-CI-executable proving command is described that asserts the four named fragments are produced and concatenated correctly. The fragment names and the concatenation step cannot be exercised by PR CI (no release job runs), making this AC post-sprint-only for the merge step.
findings.location: AC6 — release evidence artifact
findings.suggested_fix: In release-lint.yml, add: `grep -E 'evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts' .github/workflows/release.yml | wc -l` asserting ≥ 4 matches, and assert a download-artifact step name for each fragment exists in a consolidation job block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare explicit `permissions:` blocks for every new/modified job (`mcp_verify`, `mcp_registry`, and any aggregation/evidence jobs), not just the `id-token: write` grant for `mcp_registry` — e.g., `mcp_verify` should declare `permissions: {}` or the minimum required (read-only contents at most) and the `release-lint.yml` AC-5 check should assert the full permissions map, not only the presence/absence of `id-token: write`.
findings.finding: The plan declares `id-token: write` for `mcp_registry` but does not specify explicit `permissions:` blocks for the new `mcp_verify` job or any of the other jobs it creates/modifies. If the workflow carries broad top-level permissions (e.g., `contents: write` for release-asset creation, which is common in release workflows), every new job inherits them unless explicitly overridden. This leaves the access posture of `mcp_verify` undeclared for a new boundary-crossing component — the exact failure mode the T5c criterion identifies.
findings.location: Scope section / AC 5 (`mcp_verify` / `mcp_registry` job split)
findings.suggested_fix: Add explicit `permissions:` blocks in the plan's scope/AC language: `mcp_verify: permissions: {}` (or `contents: read` if needed for checkout), `mcp_registry: permissions: { id-token: write, contents: read }`. Extend the `release-lint.yml` assertion (check (e)) to verify that `mcp_verify` declares a fully-enumerated, minimal permissions block — not merely that `id-token: write` is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a happy-path assertion to `test-mcp-verify.yml` (or a separate named step in AC5) that downloads the unmodified pinned archive, runs the full verify+extract+chmod sequence, and asserts that `./mcp-publisher --version` (or `--help`) exits zero — proving the binary is correctly extracted and executable without requiring OIDC credentials.
findings.finding: The `test-mcp-verify.yml` workflow only tests the NEGATIVE case (one corrupt byte → sha256sum exits non-zero). No described step exercises the happy path — i.e., that a correctly verified archive produces a functional binary that the OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job can actually execute (`./mcp-publisher login` / `publish`). Per E6(f), a cutover to a new privileged code path requires at least one AC that exercises it end-to-end as it runs in production; a test that passes only on the reject branch does not prove the new verify-then-execute path works.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5; `.github/workflows/test-mcp-verify.yml` description
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test-mcp-verify.yml` with a second job `test-mcp-happy-path` that: (1) downloads the real pinned archive, (2) runs sha256sum -c, (3) extracts with tar xzf, (4) runs chmod +x mcp-publisher, (5) asserts `./mcp-publisher --version` exits 0. This exercises the structural path without OIDC scope.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
